prompt: How do I add a method to `f64` to calculate percentages?
broken_code: impl f64 { fn percent(&self, p: f64) -> f64 { self * (p / 100.0) } }
error_message: cannot define inherent `impl` for primitive types
fixed_code: pub trait F64Ext { fn percent(&self, p: f64) -> f64; } impl F64Ext for f64 { fn percent(&self, p: f64) -> f64 { self * (p / 100.0) } }
explanation: You cannot add inherent methods to foreign types or primitives. You must define an extension trait (like F64Ext) and implement it for the type.

prompt: I want to add a `truncate_words` method to `String`.
broken_code: impl String { fn truncate_words(&mut self, n: usize) { /* ... */ } }
error_message: cannot define inherent `impl` for a type outside of the crate where the type is defined
fixed_code: pub trait StringExt { fn truncate_words(&mut self, n: usize); } impl StringExt for String { fn truncate_words(&mut self, n: usize) { /* ... */ } }
explanation: The Orphan Rule prevents defining inherent implementations on types from other crates. Define a local extension trait and implement it.

prompt: Why can't I call my extension method in another module?
broken_code: mod ext { pub trait BoolExt { fn toggle(&mut self); } impl BoolExt for bool { fn toggle(&mut self) { *self = !*self; } } } fn main() { let mut b = true; b.toggle(); }
error_message: no method named `toggle` found for type `bool` in the current scope
fixed_code: mod ext { pub trait BoolExt { fn toggle(&mut self); } impl BoolExt for bool { fn toggle(&mut self) { *self = !*self; } } } fn main() { use ext::BoolExt; let mut b = true; b.toggle(); }
explanation: Extension trait methods are only available if the trait itself is brought into scope using a `use` statement.

prompt: I'm adding an extension method to all Iterators, but it says it's not implemented.
broken_code: trait IteratorExt { fn print_all(self); } impl<T> IteratorExt for T { fn print_all(self) { for item in self { println!("{}", item); } } }
error_message: `T` is not an iterator
fixed_code: use std::fmt::Display; trait IteratorExt { fn print_all(self); } impl<I: Iterator> IteratorExt for I where I::Item: Display { fn print_all(self) { for item in self { println!("{}", item); } } }
explanation: When writing a blanket implementation for an extension trait over Iterators, you must constrain the generic type `I` with the `Iterator` bound.

broken_code: trait SliceExt<T> { fn mid(&self) -> Option<&T>; } impl<T> SliceExt<T> for Vec<T> { fn mid(&self) -> Option<&T> { self.get(self.len() / 2) } } fn check(s: &[i32]) { s.mid(); }
error_message: no method named `mid` found for reference `&[i32]`
fixed_code: trait SliceExt<T> { fn mid(&self) -> Option<&T>; } impl<T> SliceExt<T> for [T] { fn mid(&self) -> Option<&T> { self.get(self.len() / 2) } } fn check(s: &[i32]) { s.mid(); }
explanation: To make an extension method available on both slices and Vecs (via Deref coercion), implement the extension trait directly on the slice type `[T]`.

broken_code: trait ResultExt { fn log_err(self) -> Self; } impl<T, E> ResultExt for Result<T, E> { fn log_err(self) -> Self { if let Err(e) = &self { println!("Error"); } self } }
error_message: the size for values of type `Self` cannot be known at compilation time
fixed_code: trait ResultExt: Sized { fn log_err(self) -> Self; } impl<T, E> ResultExt for Result<T, E> { fn log_err(self) -> Self { if let Err(_) = &self { println!("Error"); } self } }
explanation: When an extension trait takes `self` by value and returns `Self`, the trait must be bounded by `Sized` to ensure the compiler knows its size.

prompt: Extension method conflicts with inherent method.
broken_code: trait VecExt { fn push(&mut self, val: i32); } impl VecExt for Vec<i32> { fn push(&mut self, val: i32) { println!("ext"); } } fn main() { let mut v = vec![1]; v.push(2); }
error_message: Does not call the extension trait method
fixed_code: trait VecExt { fn push_ext(&mut self, val: i32); } impl VecExt for Vec<i32> { fn push_ext(&mut self, val: i32) { println!("ext"); } } fn main() { let mut v = vec![1]; v.push_ext(2); }
explanation: Inherent methods on a type always take precedence over trait methods with the same name. Rename the extension method to avoid the collision.

broken_code: trait OptionExt<T> { fn unwrap_or_log(self, msg: &str) -> T; } impl OptionExt<T> for Option<T> { fn unwrap_or_log(self, msg: &str) -> T { match self { Some(v) => v, None => panic!("{}", msg), } } }
error_message: type parameters must be constrained to match other types
fixed_code: trait OptionExt<T> { fn unwrap_or_log(self, msg: &str) -> T; } impl<T> OptionExt<T> for Option<T> { fn unwrap_or_log(self, msg: &str) -> T { match self { Some(v) => v, None => panic!("{}", msg), } } }
explanation: When implementing a generic extension trait, you must declare the generic parameters on the `impl` block (`impl<T> OptionExt<T> ...`).

prompt: Extending a standard library I/O trait.
broken_code: use std::io::Read; trait ReadExt { fn read_u8(&mut self) -> std::io::Result<u8>; } impl ReadExt for Read { fn read_u8(&mut self) -> std::io::Result<u8> { let mut buf = [0; 1]; self.read_exact(&mut buf)?; Ok(buf[0]) } }
error_message: the trait `Read` cannot be made into an object
fixed_code: use std::io::Read; trait ReadExt { fn read_u8(&mut self) -> std::io::Result<u8>; } impl<R: Read + ?Sized> ReadExt for R { fn read_u8(&mut self) -> std::io::Result<u8> { let mut buf = [0; 1]; self.read_exact(&mut buf)?; Ok(buf[0]) } }
explanation: You cannot implement an extension trait directly on another trait (`Read`) like a concrete type. You must use a blanket implementation over all types `R` that implement `Read`.

prompt: Using a trait extension for Futures.
broken_code: use std::future::Future; trait FutureExt { async fn log_done(self); } impl<F: Future> FutureExt for F { async fn log_done(self) { self.await; println!("Done"); } }
error_message: functions in traits cannot be declared `async`
fixed_code: use std::future::Future; trait FutureExt: Future { fn log_done(self) -> impl Future<Output = ()>; } impl<F: Future> FutureExt for F { fn log_done(self) -> impl Future<Output = ()> { async move { self.await; println!("Done"); } } }
explanation: Prior to native async traits (or when avoiding them for compatibility), you can return `impl Future` from the extension trait method.

broken_code: trait CharExt { fn is_vowel(&self) -> bool; } impl CharExt for char { fn is_vowel(self) -> bool { matches!(self, 'a'|'e'|'i'|'o'|'u') } }
error_message: method `is_vowel` has an incompatible type for trait
fixed_code: trait CharExt { fn is_vowel(&self) -> bool; } impl CharExt for char { fn is_vowel(&self) -> bool { matches!(*self, 'a'|'e'|'i'|'o'|'u') } }
explanation: The signature in the `impl` block must exactly match the signature in the trait definition. If the trait takes `&self`, the impl must take `&self`.

broken_code: trait NumExt { fn is_zero(&self) -> bool; } impl<T: PartialEq> NumExt for T { fn is_zero(&self) -> bool { *self == 0 } }
error_message: mismatched types: expected type parameter `T`, found integer
fixed_code: use std::cmp::PartialEq; trait NumExt { fn is_zero(&self) -> bool; } impl<T: PartialEq<i32>> NumExt for T { fn is_zero(&self) -> bool { *self == 0 } }
explanation: To compare a generic type `T` to `0`, `T` must implement `PartialEq<i32>`, or you must use a numeric trait crate like `num_traits::Zero`.

prompt: Calling an extension method on a reference.
broken_code: trait StrExt { fn shout(self) -> String; } impl StrExt for &str { fn shout(self) -> String { self.to_uppercase() } } fn main() { let s = String::from("hi"); s.shout(); }
error_message: no method named `shout` found for struct `String`
fixed_code: trait StrExt { fn shout(&self) -> String; } impl StrExt for str { fn shout(&self) -> String { self.to_uppercase() } } fn main() { let s = String::from("hi"); s.shout(); }
explanation: Implement extension traits for the unsized type `str` taking `&self`, rather than implementing it for `&str` taking `self`. This allows Deref coercion from `String` to work naturally.

broken_code: use std::collections::HashMap; trait MapExt { fn insert_new(&mut self, k: K, v: V); } impl<K, V> MapExt for HashMap<K, V> { fn insert_new(&mut self, k: K, v: V) { self.entry(k).or_insert(v); } }
error_message: cannot find type `K` in this scope
fixed_code: use std::collections::HashMap; trait MapExt<K, V> { fn insert_new(&mut self, k: K, v: V); } impl<K: std::hash::Hash + Eq, V> MapExt<K, V> for HashMap<K, V> { fn insert_new(&mut self, k: K, v: V) { self.entry(k).or_insert(v); } }
explanation: The trait definition itself needs generic parameters if its methods use them, or you can use associated types. Also, HashMap keys require `Hash + Eq`.

broken_code: trait ExtractExt { type Out; fn extract(&self) -> Self::Out; } impl ExtractExt for String { fn extract(&self) -> Self::Out { self.clone() } }
error_message: associated type `Out` not found for `String`
fixed_code: trait ExtractExt { type Out; fn extract(&self) -> Self::Out; } impl ExtractExt for String { type Out = String; fn extract(&self) -> Self::Out { self.clone() } }
explanation: When implementing an extension trait with an associated type, you must specify the concrete type for that associated type in the `impl` block.

broken_code: trait PointExt { fn distance(&self) -> f64; } impl PointExt for (f64, f64) { fn distance(self) -> f64 { (self.0.powi(2) + self.1.powi(2)).sqrt() } }
error_message: method `distance` has an incompatible type for trait
fixed_code: trait PointExt { fn distance(&self) -> f64; } impl PointExt for (f64, f64) { fn distance(&self) -> f64 { (self.0.powi(2) + self.1.powi(2)).sqrt() } }
explanation: The self parameter in the trait definition (`&self`) must match the implementation. Tuples can have extension traits implemented for them just like structs.

broken_code: trait StringIterExt { fn chars_twice(&self) -> impl Iterator<Item = char>; } impl StringIterExt for String { fn chars_twice(&self) -> impl Iterator<Item = char> { self.chars().chain(self.chars()) } }
error_message: return type cannot contain a projection or `Self` that references lifetimes from a parent scope
fixed_code: trait StringIterExt { fn chars_twice(&self) -> impl Iterator<Item = char> + '_; } impl StringIterExt for String { fn chars_twice(&self) -> impl Iterator<Item = char> + '_ { self.chars().chain(self.chars()) } }
explanation: When returning `impl Trait` that captures a lifetime from `&self`, you must explicitly bind it using `+ '_` in the trait signature.

broken_code: mod my_mod { trait NumberExt { fn double(&self) -> Self; } impl NumberExt for i32 { fn double(&self) -> Self { self * 2 } } } fn main() { use my_mod::NumberExt; let x = 5.double(); }
error_message: trait `NumberExt` is private
fixed_code: mod my_mod { pub trait NumberExt { fn double(&self) -> Self; } impl NumberExt for i32 { fn double(&self) -> Self { self * 2 } } } fn main() { use my_mod::NumberExt; let x = 5.double(); }
explanation: Extension traits must be declared `pub` to be imported and used outside the module where they are defined.

prompt: Creating an extension for a smart pointer.
broken_code: use std::rc::Rc; trait RcExt { fn is_shared(&self) -> bool; } impl RcExt for Rc<T> { fn is_shared(&self) -> bool { Rc::strong_count(self) > 1 } }
error_message: cannot find type `T` in this scope
fixed_code: use std::rc::Rc; trait RcExt { fn is_shared(&self) -> bool; } impl<T> RcExt for Rc<T> { fn is_shared(&self) -> bool { Rc::strong_count(self) > 1 } }
explanation: When extending a generic smart pointer like `Rc<T>`, you must declare the generic parameter `T` on the `impl` block.

broken_code: trait ClearExt { fn clear_all(&mut self); } impl<T> ClearExt for Vec<T> { fn clear_all(self) { self.clear(); } }
error_message: method `clear_all` has an incompatible type for trait
fixed_code: trait ClearExt { fn clear_all(&mut self); } impl<T> ClearExt for Vec<T> { fn clear_all(&mut self) { self.clear(); } }
explanation: If the trait method dictates `&mut self`, the implementation must also accept `&mut self`, not `self` by value.

broken_code: trait ArrayExt { fn sum_elements(&self) -> i32; } impl ArrayExt for [i32; N] { fn sum_elements(&self) -> i32 { self.iter().sum() } }
error_message: cannot find value `N` in this scope
fixed_code: trait ArrayExt { fn sum_elements(&self) -> i32; } impl<const N: usize> ArrayExt for [i32; N] { fn sum_elements(&self) -> i32 { self.iter().sum() } }
explanation: To implement an extension trait for an array of any size, you must use const generics (`<const N: usize>`).

prompt: Extension traits with trait bounds on methods.
broken_code: trait PrintExt { fn print_if_debug(&self); } impl<T> PrintExt for T { fn print_if_debug(&self) { println!("{:?}", self); } }
error_message: `T` doesn't implement `Debug`
fixed_code: use std::fmt::Debug; trait PrintExt { fn print_if_debug(&self); } impl<T: Debug> PrintExt for T { fn print_if_debug(&self) { println!("{:?}", self); } }
explanation: If your blanket implementation uses a feature like `println!("{:?}")`, you must bound the generic parameter `T` with `Debug`.

broken_code: trait RefExt { fn is_null_ref(&self) -> bool; } impl<T> RefExt for &T { fn is_null_ref(&self) -> bool { false } }
error_message: type parameter `T` must be covered by another type when it appears before the first local type (`RefExt`)
fixed_code: trait RefExt { fn is_null_ref(&self) -> bool; } impl<T> RefExt for *const T { fn is_null_ref(&self) -> bool { self.is_null() } }
explanation: You cannot implement a local trait for a generic remote type like `&T` without running into orphan rule restrictions unless you provide local bounds. Raw pointers or custom types are alternatives.

prompt: Implementing an extension trait on a trait object.
broken_code: use std::io::Read; trait ReadExt { fn ready(&self) -> bool; } impl ReadExt for dyn Read { fn ready(&self) -> bool { true } } fn check(r: &std::fs::File) { r.ready(); }
error_message: no method named `ready` found for reference `&File`
fixed_code: use std::io::Read; trait ReadExt { fn ready(&self) -> bool; } impl<R: Read> ReadExt for R { fn ready(&self) -> bool { true } } fn check(r: &std::fs::File) { r.ready(); }
explanation: Implementing an extension on `dyn Read` only applies to trait objects. To apply it to all types that implement `Read` (like `File`), use a blanket generic impl.

prompt: Using Self in an extension trait.
broken_code: trait BuilderExt { fn with_name(self, name: String) -> Self; } impl BuilderExt for std::process::Command { fn with_name(mut self, name: String) -> Self { self.arg(name); self } }
error_message: cannot return reference to local variable `self`
fixed_code: trait BuilderExt { fn with_name(self, name: String) -> Self; } impl BuilderExt for std::process::Command { fn with_name(mut self, name: String) -> Self { self.arg(name); self } }
explanation: If your extension method takes `self` by value and returns `Self` to allow method chaining (builder pattern), ensure you return the value itself, not a reference.
